fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out a Google Calendar integration library for our platform. Basically developers are spending way too much time hand-rolling all the plumbing to talk to Google Calendar — auth flows, fetching events, creating/updating them, checking availability, etc. It's a mess and every developer is doing it slightly differently which causes bugs.

The library should handle the full lifecycle: getting users connected (similar to how we did the login flow on the old CRM project, you know the one), reading and writing calendar data, searching events, and checking when people are free or busy. It also needs to handle errors gracefully — no one wants raw stack traces bubbling up to the UI.

There's also some nuance around how events are structured — all-day events behave differently, there are reminder configurations, recurrence rules, custom properties you can attach, guest permission flags, that kind of thing. The serialization to wire format needs to be exact or the Google API will reject it.

The adapter should be drivable from structured JSON commands so we can test it in isolation. Errors should come back as normalized category strings, not raw HTTP codes or exception class names. We also need calendar list enumeration and free/busy querying for the scheduling feature. Please make sure the URL encoding and query parameter ordering matches what we agreed on — the QA team flagged that last time as a blocker.

One more pass on the specifics the team asked about. For the consent link, the authorization URL needs to be exactly https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/auth and the query params have to stay in this exact order: access_type=offline, client_id=<value>, redirect_uri=<value>, response_type=code, scope=https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar. That scope needs to be exactly https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar, not calendar.readonly and not any other variant. Same story on the build_consent_link behavior we talked about before: the fixed ordering is access_type, client_id, redirect_uri, response_type, scope, and we need to keep that stable.

Also, after the one-time code exchange, we should not keep that one-time authorization code around as reusable client state. The one exception is that the black-box adapter still echoes it back in stdout as auth_code_after_exchange=<original one_time_code>, but that’s only there for deterministic reporting, not because we’re storing it as active state.

On availability/transparency, if the input is null or just not provided, we default to 'opaque'. In that case the output needs to include exactly these three lines: transparency=opaque, opaque=true, transparent=false. If the mode is 'transparent', then it should be transparency=transparent, opaque=false, transparent=true.

For the reminder payload task, the reminder JSON gets printed twice on two separate lines, and both lines are identical. The JSON key order matters: overrides first, then useDefault. Inside overrides, each object also needs alphabetical key order as {method, minutes}. The example to follow is exactly {"overrides":[{"method":"popup","minutes":6}],"useDefault":false}\n{"overrides":[{"method":"popup","minutes":6}],"useDefault":false}\n

For calendar list enumeration, the endpoint is GET /users/me/calendarList. Each returned entry needs to expose id, summary, time_zone, access_role, primary, and calendar_id, where calendar_id equals the entry id. The primary value should be true/false as a boolean, with false for non-primary calendars and true for the primary one.

For free/busy, the request is a POST to /freeBusy with body: {"items": [{"id": "<cal_id>"}, ...], "timeMax": "<end>", "timeMin": "<start>"}. Keep the keys in alphabetical order. The output should include the request details, then calendar_ids=<comma-separated list>, then for each calendar <cal_id>.busy_count=<n>, and for each busy block <cal_id>.busy<i>.start=<value> and <cal_id>.busy<i>.end=<value>. If a calendar has no busy windows, it should only show busy_count=0.

On error handling, HTTP failures need to come back as http_status=<code>, error=<normalized_category>, and details=<raw upstream response body as-is multiline string>. The details part is literally the raw upstream body printed verbatim, not re-serialized into something nicer. And error should always be a snake_case category like bad_request for 400 or not_found for 404, never just the numeric code and never an exception class name.

A couple request-flow details too: deleting an event is a two-step sequence. First we do a POST to create it, then a DELETE to /calendars/<encoded_cal_id>/events/<event_id>?sendNotifications=false. The output needs created.id=<id> before the POST request details, and after the DELETE succeeds we show after_delete.id= with the value left empty to reflect that the local identifier was cleared.

For update-or-create, there are two numbered requests: request0 is a GET to fetch the existing event by identifier, and request1 is a PUT to update it. The PUT has to target the event’s real id from the fetched record, not the lookup identifier, and the PUT JSON body also includes the id field. That PUT path ends with ?sendNotifications=false.

Last thing, a couple encoding and ordering details that QA was rightly picky about: @ needs to become %40 in calendar ID path segments, = needs to become %3D in extended property values, and for time-window queries the parameter order stays fixed as timeMin, timeMax, orderBy, maxResults, singleEvents.

trigger_keywords: missing credentials error format
blank client id error output
credential validation error string
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When required credential fields are absent or blank, the output must be exactly: error=missing_credentials\n — no exception class name, no stack trace, no HTTP status code. This must be a single normalized line.

trigger_keywords: calendar profile update request body
PUT body JSON field ordering
calendar write serialization format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The request body for a calendar profile update (PUT) must be a JSON object with exactly these fields in alphabetical key order: description, location, summary, timeZone. Fields not supplied in the change set must appear as null (not omitted). Example: {"description":"Work event list","location":null,"summary":"Our Company","timeZone":null}.

trigger_keywords: calendar ID URL encoding
percent encoding of at-sign in calendar path
@ symbol encoding in event endpoint
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Calendar IDs containing the @ symbol must be percent-encoded as %40 when embedded in URL paths (e.g., /calendars/klei8jnelo09nflqehnvfzipgs%40group.calendar.google.com/events). However, calendar IDs that do not contain @ (e.g., in profile endpoints like /calendars/gqeb0i6v737kfu5md0f35htjlg@group.calendar.google.com) appear without encoding in those specific endpoints. The encoding rule applies consistently to the events sub-path calendar ID.

trigger_keywords: text search query parameter passthrough
search query URL not encoded
q parameter raw value in path
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When searching events with a text query, the query string is appended to the path as-is after ?q= without additional URL encoding. For example, a query of 'Test&gsessionid=12345' produces a path ending in ?q=Test&gsessionid=12345. The query value is NOT percent-encoded.

trigger_keywords: time window query parameter ordering
events in window URL parameters
timeMin timeMax query string order
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For time-window event queries, the query parameters must appear in this exact order: timeMin, timeMax, orderBy=startTime, maxResults=25, singleEvents=true. No other parameters should be present unless a text query (q=) is also specified.

trigger_keywords: custom property query encoding
extended property equals sign encoding
sharedExtendedProperty URL format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Custom property filters use repeated query parameters. For shared properties, each key-value pair becomes sharedExtendedProperty=key%3Dvalue (the = between key and value is percent-encoded as %3D). For private properties, use privateExtendedProperty=key%3Dvalue. Multiple properties produce multiple repeated parameters. The full parameter set also includes orderBy=startTime&maxResults=25&singleEvents=true.

trigger_keywords: event JSON wire format field order
event serialization key ordering
event payload JSON structure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Event JSON payloads must serialize keys in strict alphabetical order. Required fields always present: description, end (as {"dateTime": ...}), guestsCanInviteOthers, guestsCanSeeOtherGuests, location, reminders, start (as {"dateTime": ...}), summary, transparency, visibility. Optional fields (attendees, extendedProperties, id, recurrence) are included only when provided. The default visibility is 'default' and default transparency is 'opaque'. Default reminders is {"useDefault": true}.

trigger_keywords: all-day event detection
date-only boundary all day flag
all_day duration seconds calculation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An event is considered all-day when its start and end values are date-only strings (format YYYY-MM-DD, no time component). The output must include all_day=true and duration_seconds=<value> where the value is the number of seconds between start and end dates. For a single-day event (e.g., 2012-03-31 to 2012-04-01), duration_seconds=86400.

trigger_keywords: event creation POST path query parameter
sendNotifications flag on create
event creation endpoint format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Event creation uses POST to /calendars/<encoded_cal_id>/events?sendNotifications=false. The result output includes result.id, result.title, result.description, and result.location lines (not request-prefixed). Quick-add events use a different path format with a text parameter.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: calendar dispatcher task field
google calendar adapter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The adapter routes on the input field 'task'. Valid task values are the task names shown in public_test_cases, such as build_consent_link, exchange_one_time_code, missing_credentials, read_calendar_profile, change_calendar_profile, list_events, search_events, events_in_window, events_with_custom_properties, event_by_identifier, create_detailed_event, delete_created_event, calendar_list_entries, free_busy_query, and http_error_response.

how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: errors should come back as normalized category strings
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The error output format defined in feature7_1_http_error_mapping.json: three output lines — http_status=<code>, error=<snake_case_category>, details=<raw_body> — where the category maps HTTP status codes to strings like bad_request, not_found, etc.

context_pointers.vague_reference: The serialization to wire format needs to be exact or the Google API will reject it
context_pointers.actual_meaning: JSON keys must be in strict alphabetical order for all serialized payloads (event bodies, calendar profile bodies, free/busy request bodies, reminder objects), as demonstrated across feature4_1, feature5_4, feature5_5, and feature6_2 test cases

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented here. Please check the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the expected input/output contract, or look at the upstream response fixture data to understand what the service returns. If it's about a specific field format or ordering, the test cases are the authoritative source.

feature1_1_oauth_consent_url.json.description: Build the browser authorization URL for offline calendar access from client credentials, redirect URL, and optional state.

feature1_2_oauth_token_exchange.json.description: Exchange an authorization code or refresh token and expose the resulting tokens without retaining the one-time code.

feature1_3_credentials_required.json.description: Reject connection setup when the required client credential fields are absent or blank.

feature2_1_calendar_profile_retrieval.js.description: Retrieve a calendar profile and render the request endpoint plus profile fields returned by the service.

cases.expected_output:
request0.method=get
request0.path=/calendars/gqeb0i6v737kfu5md0f35htjlg@group.calendar.google.com
id=gqeb0i6v737kfu5md0f35htjlg@group.calendar.google.com
summary=Some Calendar
description=Our work events
location=Portland
time_zone=America/Los_Angeles

request0.body={"description":"Work event list","location":null,"summary":"Our Company","timeZone":null}
id=gqeb0i6v737kfu5md0f35htjlg@group.calendar.google.com
summary=Our Company
description=Work event list

feature4_1_event_creation.json.description: Create events from detailed fields or quick natural-language text and render request details plus the saved event identity.

cases.expected_output:
request0.method=post
request0.path=/calendars/klei8jnelo09nflqehnvfzipgs%40group.calendar.google.com/events?sendNotifications=false
request0.body={"description":"A new event","end":{"dateTime":"2014-11-16T21:17:31-08:00"},"extendedProperties":{"shared":{"key":"value"}},"guestsCanInviteOthers":null,"guestsCanSeeOtherGuests":null,"location":"Joe's House","reminders":{"useDefault":true},"start":{"dateTime":"2014-11-16T20:17:31-08:00"},"summary":"New Event","transparency":"opaque","visibility":"default"}
result.id=fhru34kt6ikmr20knd2456l08n
result.title=New Event

feature4_3_event_deletion.json.description: Delete a saved event through the event endpoint and clear the local event identifier after successful deletion.

feature5_1_event_identifier_validation.j.description: Accept valid event identifiers and normalize invalid identifier errors without leaking runtime exception names.

feature5_2_all_day_events.json.description: Determine and assign all-day events using date-only boundaries and render date-only payload fields for all-day events.

feature5_3_availability_and_creator_fiel.description: Normalize event transparency inputs into availability modes and expose creator display names from service records.
